content.text:
A ring homomorphism preserves both operations—addition and 
multiplication. The theory parallels group homomorphisms, with ideals 
playing the role of normal subgroups.

content.title: Ring Homomorphism
content.content:
A function $\phi: R \to S$ between rings is a **ring 
homomorphism** if for all $a, b \in R$:

1. $\phi(a + b) = \phi(a) + \phi(b)$

2. $\phi(ab) = \phi(a)\phi(b)$
If $R$ and $S$ have unity and $\phi(1_R) = 1_S$, then $\phi$ is a 
**unital homomorphism**.

content.title: Ring homomorphisms
content.problem:
- $\phi: \Z \to \Z_n$ by $\phi(k) = k \mod n$ (unital)

- $\phi: \R[x] \to \R$ by $\phi(f) = f(3)$ (evaluation at $3$)

- $\phi: \C \to \C$ by $\phi(z) = \bar{z}$ (conjugation)

- $\phi: R \to R[x]$ by $\phi(r) = r$ (constant polynomial)

content.title: Kernel is an Ideal
content.content:
If $\phi: R \to S$ is a ring homomorphism, then $\ker(\phi) = \{r \in R : 
\phi(r) = 0\}$ is an ideal of $R$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We know $\ker(\phi)$ is an additive subgroup. For absorption: if 
$a \in \ker(\phi)$ and $r \in R$, then 
$\phi(ra) = \phi(r)\phi(a) = \phi(r) \cdot 0 = 0$, so $ra \in \ker(\phi)$.

content.title: First Isomorphism Theorem
content.content:
If $\phi: R \to S$ is a ring homomorphism, then:
\[
 R/\ker(\phi) \cong \text{Im}(\phi)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Application
content.problem:
The evaluation map $\phi: \R[x] \to \C$ defined by $\phi(f) = f(i)$ 
has kernel $(x^2 + 1)$ and image $\C$. Thus:
\[
 \R[x]/(x^2 + 1) \cong \C
\]

content.title: Ideals are Kernels
content.content:
A subset $I \subseteq R$ is an ideal if and only if $I = \ker(\phi)$ 
for some ring homomorphism $\phi: R \to S$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Leftarrow)$ Proved above.

$(\Rightarrow)$ If $I$ is an ideal, the quotient map $\pi: R \to R/I$ 
defined by $\pi(r) = r + I$ is a homomorphism with $\ker(\pi) = I$.

items:
Show that $\phi: \Z[x] \to \Z$ by $\phi(f) = f(0)$ is a 
 homomorphism. Find its kernel.
Find all ring homomorphisms from $\Z_6$ to $\Z_{15}$.

If $\phi: R \to S$ is a surjective ring homomorphism and $R$ is 
 commutative, prove $S$ is commutative.

Show that $\Z[\sqrt{-5}]/(2, 1 + \sqrt{-5})$ is a field with 
 two elements.

marginNotes.text:
The Word became flesh and made his dwelling 
among us.
